solution: Simplify first: \( f(x)=[[a]]x+[[b]] \), so \( f'(x)=[[a]] \) for all \( x \), giving \( [[answer]] \).
By the Pythagorean identity, \( \sin^{2}x+\cos^{2}x=1 \) for all \( x \), so \( f(x)=[[a]]x+[[b]]+1 \) and \( f'(x)=[[a]] \) identically, giving \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats the constant \( 1+b \) as if it survived differentiation with a leftover \( a+b \) term.
This differentiates \( \sin^2x+\cos^2x \) term-by-term as if it were \( x^2 \)-like instead of recognizing the identity collapses it to 1.
This drops the constant slope \( a \) contribution entirely.
This assumes the whole function is constant, missing the \( ax \) term.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

solution: By log properties, \( \ln([[a]]x)-\ln(x)=\ln[[a]] \), a constant, so \( f(x)=\ln[[a]]+[[b]]x \) and \( f'(x)=[[b]] \) identically, giving \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats the constant \( \ln a \) term as if it still contributed \( 1/x_0 \) to the derivative.
This differentiates \( \ln(ax) \) alone without cancelling against \( -\ln(x) \).
This confuses \( \ln(ax) \)'s derivative with \( a/x \) instead of \( 1/x \).
This flips the sign of the leftover logarithmic term, which should cancel to zero.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

solution: Since \( e^{\ln x + a} = x\,e^{a} \), we get \( f(x)=e^{[[a]]}x+[[b]]x=(e^{[[a]]}+[[b]])x \), so \( f'(x)=e^{[[a]]}+[[b]] \) identically, giving \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is missing the extra factor of \( x \) that survives from \( e^{\ln x+a}=xe^a \), so it drops the \( e^a \) slope term.
This ignores the \( e^{\ln x + a} \) term entirely, differentiating only \( bx \).
This treats \( e^{\ln x+a} \) as if it collapsed to the linear term \( ax \) instead of \( e^a x \).
This evaluates the collapsed slope at \( x_0 \) as if it were not constant, multiplying by \( x_0 \) again.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

solution: Expanding the cubes, \( (x+[[a]])^{3}-(x-[[a]])^{3}=6\cdot[[a]]x^{2}+2\cdot[[a]]^{3} \), so \( f(x)=6x^{2}+2\cdot[[a]]^{2} \) after dividing by \( [[a]] \). Then \( f'(x)=12x \), so \( f'([[x0]])=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the factor of 2 from the correct coefficient, using \( 6x_0 \) instead of \( 12x_0 \).
This differentiates with respect to \( a \) instead of \( x \).
This forgets to apply the power rule's coefficient, treating \( f(x)=6x^2 \) as if it were linear.
This is the constant term \( 2a^{2} \) left over after dividing, mistaken for the derivative.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

solution: By the double-angle identity, \( 2\sin x\cos x=\sin(2x) \), so \( f(x)=\sin(2x)+[[a]]x \) and \( f'(x)=2\cos(2x)+[[a]] \). At \( x=[[x0]] \) this is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the \( 2\cos(2x) \) contribution entirely, keeping only the linear slope \( a \).
This uses \( \frac{d}{dx}\sin(2x)=2 \) (a constant) instead of \( 2\cos(2x) \).
This forgets the factor of 2 from the chain rule on \( \sin(2x) \).
This uses the derivative of \( \cos(2x) \) instead of \( \sin(2x) \), picking up the wrong sign and function.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
